title: Lippia hastulata
intro: Lippia hastulata, also known as Chilean verbena or lippia, is a flowering plant species native to South America, characterized by its small, white or pink flowers and fragrant leaves. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal effects, which have been attributed to its ability to help alleviate symptoms of colds, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Lippia hastulata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. In traditional medicine, Lippia hastulata is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is commonly used to treat respiratory infections and skin conditions, as well as to promote relaxation and reduce stress.
taxonomy: Lippia hastulata is classified within the family Verbenaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the verbena family. This family is further classified under the order Lamiales, which includes other related families such as Lamiaceae and Acanthaceae. The order Lamiales is part of the clade Asterids, which is a larger group of flowering plants. The clade Asterids is a subgroup of the larger clade Angiosperm, which includes all flowering plants. Lippia hastulata's classification is also sometimes expressed using the APG III system as follows: Lippia hastulata Verbenaceae Lamiales Asterids Angiosperm.
